query: What is the optimal door-to-balloon time for STEMI and how does delay affect mortality?

pico.population: Adult patients presenting with ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)
pico.intervention: Primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) with varying door-to-balloon times
pico.comparison: Different time intervals from hospital arrival to balloon inflation

papers.title: Door-to-Balloon Time and Mortality among Patients Undergoing Primary PCI
papers.key_finding: Longer door-to-balloon times were associated with higher in-hospital mortality. Each 30-minute delay increased mortality risk by approximately 7.5%.

papers.title: 2017 ESC Guidelines for the management of acute myocardial infarction in patients presenting with ST-segment elevation
papers.key_finding: Target door-to-balloon time should be ≤60 minutes at PCI-capable hospitals. Maximum acceptable delay is 120 minutes from STEMI diagnosis.

papers.title: An Updated Meta-Analysis on Door-to-Balloon Time and Mortality in STEMI
papers.key_finding: Despite dramatic reductions in door-to-balloon time nationally, in-hospital mortality for STEMI did not decline proportionally, suggesting total ischemic time and system delays also matter.

synthesis: The optimal door-to-balloon (D2B) time for STEMI is ≤90 minutes per ACC/AHA guidelines and ≤60 minutes per ESC guidelines. Multiple large studies demonstrate that each 30-minute delay in reperfusion increases mortality by approximately 7.5%. The absolute benefit of shorter D2B times is greatest in the first 2 hours. Current quality measures target system-level D2B times ≤90 minutes for at least 75% of STEMI patients. However, total ischemic time (symptom onset to reperfusion) may be more important than D2B time alone.
